common.title: Affirmations — A Gentle Companion
common.introText1: If you feel comfortable, you may choose to stand in front of a mirror while reading these affirmations.
common.introText2: You might gently look at yourself and say each line out loud, slowly and clearly. Hearing your own voice can make the words feel more personal and grounding.
common.noRightWay: There's no right way to do this.
common.noRush: There is no need to rush or force any feeling.
common.meaningfulMoment: Even if the words don't feel fully true right now, allowing this moment of intention can still be meaningful.
common.readyToBegin: Whenever you feel ready, you may begin ✨

common.justNotice: Just notice what feels closest to your experience in this moment.

establish_a_relaxing_routine_in_5_days_s: Establish a relaxing routine in 5 days steps
hi_i_ve_just_finished_my_session_i_d_lik: Hi, I've just finished my session. I'd like to share some things I'm feeling today. Lately, I've been feeling anxious & stressed a lot CONSTANTLY.
i_m_sorry_to_hear_that_you_re_feeling_th: I'm sorry to hear that you're feeling this way. It's completely understandable to feel like this when so much is going on. Let's talk this through together.
at_my_job_my_manager_put_me_through_a_lo: At my job, my manager put me through a lot last year. I know that it's the nature of the job, maybe I wasn't cut out for it or maybe I just couldn't cope. I feel trapped here now.
i_m_here_to_help_navigate_those_feelings: I'm here to help navigate those feelings - you're not alone in this. It's really hard when you care so much about what you do but feel trapped at the same time.
let_s_explore_this_together_and_see_if_w: Let's explore this together and see if we can find some ways to cope with the stress and anxiety you're experiencing.
i_m_sensing_that_you_re_not_feeling_like: I'm sensing that you're not feeling like your "GentleSelfish" self. Can you tell me more about what you're going through? What's been triggering these anxious thoughts lately?
i_m_feeling_that_anxiety_in_a_lot_of_dif: I'm feeling that anxiety in a lot of different areas of my life. It's not just the work, it's relationships too. I feel like I'm not enough for a lot of people.
i_m_so_sorry_i_can_imagine_experiencing_: I'm so sorry. I can imagine experiencing some major and minor stressors across different areas. Anxiety can be so exhausting. Can you tell me a little bit more about the areas you're feeling this way in?
here_are_some_common_areas_themes_in_dai: Here are some common areas & themes in daily living when it comes to how to balance everyone's needs:
perhaps_you_find_it_difficult_to_say_no_: Perhaps you find it difficult to say no to extra projects or overtime, even when you're already stretched thin.
you_might_struggle_to_express_your_own_n: You might struggle to express your own needs or desires in relationships, fearing conflict or the loss of connection.
whether_it_s_taking_care_of_aging_parent: Whether it's taking care of aging parents or raising children, you feel you must put their needs above your own well-being.
maybe_you_avoid_saying_no_to_social_invi: Maybe you avoid saying "no" to social invitations out of guilt, even when you're exhausted or would prefer solitude.
yes_a_lot_of_those_felt_really_true_for_: Yes, a lot of those felt really true for me. Especially the "GentleSelfish" part. How do we work on saying "no" without guilt and feeling guilt-free about doing things that you enjoy? I tend to feel guilt easily.
that_s_a_beautiful_goal_you_ve_identifie: That's a beautiful goal you've identified - and an absolutely valuable one! While it may seem counterintuitive, prioritizing your own needs & well-being actually makes you MORE effective in caring for others, not less.
would_you_like_to_explore_strategies_to_: Would you like to explore strategies to help with this?
i_think_what_i_ve_realized_aside_from_th: I think what I've realized - aside from the fact that I don't know who I'm becoming. I don't know who I am anymore. I only know what I was or I guess what everyone wanted me to be. Do you understand what I'm trying to say?
i_think_i_can_now_fully_hear_what_you_re: I think I can now fully hear what you're saying. You want to feel like you're creating a sense of self that aligns with YOUR needs. Not a needs of other people towards you but your real needs, and maybe slowly discover who you are.
very_very_strongly_this_journey_to_me_is: Very very strongly: this journey to me is less about finding a "perfect" self, but rather being more AUTHENTIC & GENTLE with who you are becoming. You're very strong for identifying this.
i_ve_noticed_a_really_great_tool_that_yo: I've noticed a really great tool that you haven't yet tried on Mantra - it's called "GentleSelfish" and I really think you'd like what it has to offer. You're working on saying "no", managing guilt and creating space for self care. So the tool is designed to help with EXACTLY some issues you've just mentioned.
let_s_do_this_i_m_excited_about_your_pla: Let's do this. I'm excited about your plan. I'll work through these tools and update you after a couple of weeks. Is that okay?
i_m_so_glad_you_re_excited_about_your_pl: I'm so glad you're excited about your plan. I know that you haven't met yourself yet but when you do, you're going to ABSOLUTELY LOVE this person. Very very strongly: this journey to me is less about finding a "perfect" self, but rather just being more AUTHENTIC & GENTLE with who you are becoming.
it_s_also_nice_to_just_share_today_s_fee: It's also nice to just share today's feeling. I think I've never really had space to explore these thoughts or ideas before. All I ever get is judgement, and I just need to release some of that, and I guess that's what I just did.
i_m_so_glad_you_feel_like_you_ve_found_y: I'm so glad you feel like you've found your space to talk about your feelings without fear of judgment. Your space is sacred, and I really really appreciate you using this space in this special way.
if_you_again_feel_like_sharing_your_spac: If you again feel like sharing your space, you know where to find me - right here on our very special pages.
is_there_anything_else_i_can_help_you_wi: Is there anything else I can help you with today?

intro_p1: When you're feeling depressed, anxious, or overwhelmed, it's easy to lose sight of what truly matters to you.
intro_p2: A personal mission statement is not about pressure or productivity. It is a gentle reminder of your values — the kind of person you want to be, even during difficult seasons.
start_building_your_amazing_project_here: Start building your amazing project here!

steps.body:
Let's take a short pause together.

For the next minute, there's nothing you need to solve, fix, or respond to. Just allow yourself to slow down.

Sit comfortably, place your feet on the ground if you can, and gently take a slow breath in… and slowly breathe out.

This is your moment to reset and reconnect with the present.

steps.heading: 5 Things You Can See
steps.body: Slowly look around you. Let your eyes move gently across the space. Notice colors, shapes, light, shadows, or small details you may not have paid attention to before.

steps.heading: 4 Things You Can Feel
steps.body: Bring your attention to your body. Feel your feet touching the floor, your back against the chair, your clothes on your skin, or the air around you. Acknowledge four physical sensations.

steps.heading: 3 Things You Can Hear
steps.body: Listen carefully. You might hear distant sounds, a fan, typing, breathing, or even silence. Identify three sounds without judging them.

steps.heading: 2 Things You Can Smell
steps.body: See if you can detect any scent in the room. If nothing stands out, simply notice the neutral smell of the air around you.

steps.heading: 1 Thing You Can Taste
steps.body: Pay attention to the taste in your mouth. It could be subtle or neutral — just observe it.

steps.heading: Reflection
steps.body: Take one slow breath in… and gently breathe out.

app_complete_message: You've successfully recalled and connected with a source of joy. Keep this feeling with you as you move through your day.

affirmation.title: You Took a Beautiful Step
affirmation.p1: Reconnecting with what brings you joy — even in the smallest way — is an act of kindness toward yourself.
affirmation.p2: You deserve moments of lightness and peace. Keep nurturing what makes your heart feel full. 💛

welcome_subtitle: Clear Your Mind. Find Your Focus.
complete_message: Congratulations! You've successfully completed a line of self-care. Your mind and body thank you.

questions.statement: Anger is bad. If you feel angry, something is wrong with you.
questions.realFact: Anger is a normal, healthy emotion. Everyone feels it. It's not a sign that something is 'wrong' with you — it's a signal that something matters to you. What counts is how you respond to it, not whether you feel it.
questions.example: A veteran feels angry when their boundaries are crossed. That anger is valid — it's telling them something important.

questions.statement: Anger can be a signal that your boundaries have been crossed.
questions.realFact: Anger is information. When you feel it, it's worth asking: what boundary was crossed? What need isn't being met? Listening to your anger — without acting on impulse — helps you understand yourself better and take meaningful action.
questions.example: You feel angry when a coworker takes credit for your work. That anger is pointing to a need for recognition and fairness.

questions.statement: If you're angry, you have to let it out by yelling or hitting things.
questions.realFact: Healthy anger expression doesn't mean suppression OR explosion. It means acknowledging your feelings and expressing them constructively — through words, journaling, physical activity, or creative outlets. You can honor your anger without destruction.
questions.example: Instead of punching a wall, you go for a run or write down exactly what's bothering you. The anger moves through you without causing harm.

questions.statement: Learning to manage anger is a sign of emotional strength.
questions.realFact: People who work on their anger management aren't weak — they're doing some of the bravest emotional work there is. Choosing to pause, reflect, and respond thoughtfully takes far more strength than reacting on impulse.
questions.example: Taking a deep breath and saying 'I need a minute before I respond' in a heated moment is a powerful act of self-control.

questions.statement: If someone makes you angry, it's their fault. You have no responsibility.
questions.realFact: Other people can trigger your anger, but how you respond is your responsibility — and your power. Taking ownership of your reactions doesn't mean the other person was right. It means you're choosing how to show up. That's empowerment, not blame.
questions.example: Someone cuts you off in traffic. You feel angry — that's normal. But you choose whether to rage or take a breath. The choice is yours.

support_footer: If anxiety feels overwhelming or persistent, consider speaking with a mental health professional.
tip_seek_support_desc: Talk to someone you trust to gain reassurance and perspective.
tip_deep_breathing_desc: Slow breathing can calm your nervous system quickly.
tip_mindfulness_desc: Focus on the present moment without judgment.
tip_muscle_relaxation_desc: Release physical tension to ease anxiety.
tip_positive_self_talk_desc: Replace anxious thoughts with realistic, calming statements.
breathing_why: Anxiety activates the fight-or-flight response. Slow breathing signals safety to your brain and body.
breathing_step1: Inhale through your nose for 4 seconds
mindfulness_why: Anxiety often focuses on future fears. Mindfulness brings your attention back to the present moment.
muscle_why: Anxiety creates physical tension. Releasing muscles sends calming signals to your brain.
selftalk_why: Anxious thoughts often exaggerate danger. Balanced thinking reduces fear intensity.
selftalk_step4: Replace it with a realistic statement
support_why: Anxiety can make worries feel bigger than they are. Sharing them with someone trusted can reduce isolation and provide reassurance.
there_s_no_single_way_to_carry: There's no single way to carry
what_stays_with_you_in_your_own_way: What stays with you, in your own way,

technique_description: Box breathing uses a simple 4-4-4-4 pattern — inhale, hold, exhale, hold — each for four seconds. It activates your parasympathetic nervous system, lowering stress and bringing you back to a calm, focused state.
session_complete_msg: Great job completing your breathing session. Take a moment to notice how your body feels — lighter, steadier, more at ease.

screens.headline: Why Control Can Feel So Powerful
paragraphs: For many people experiencing eating difficulties, control can feel like a way to create safety.
When life feels unpredictable or emotionally intense, focusing on food, weight, or routines can bring a sense of structure and certainty.
This isn't about willpower or discipline. It's about trying to steady yourself when things feel uncertain.
In this way, control becomes a coping strategy — something that helps you feel grounded, even if only temporarily.
screens.microcopy: You're not alone in feeling this way.

screens.headline: Different Ways Control Appears
screens.intro: The need for control doesn't look the same for everyone, but it often shows up in patterns like:
screens.outro: Over time, these can become rigid, taking up mental space and making it harder to adapt.
screens.microcopy: What starts as control can slowly start to feel controlling.

screens.headline: Underneath the Control
screens.intro: The urge to control often has deeper roots. It can be connected to:

bullets.text: A need for stability and safety

screens.insight: Control isn't the problem — it's the strategy your mind found to cope. With time and support, other ways to feel safe are possible.
screens.microcopy: Understanding is a powerful first step.

intro_text_1: Gratitude does not mean ignoring difficult emotions.
intro_text_2: It simply means gently noticing moments - big or small - that feel steady, comforting, or meaningful.
intro_text_3: Some days it may be something very small.
grateful_step_2: What is one small thing you appreciate right now?
reflection_text: When you focus on this moment, what do you notice in yourself?
placeholder_feeling: When I think about this, I feel...
closing_text_1: Taking time to reflect is an act of care.
closing_text_2: You can return to this space anytime.
no_entries: Complete 3 check-ins to see your weekly trend.
how_do_you_feel_after_this: How do you feel after this?
gentleSteps: Gentle steps to help you move forward, one day at a time.
strugglingMessage: If you're struggling deeply, consider reaching out to someone you trust or a mental health professional.
t1_preview: Do small activities that bring even a tiny bit of comfort or interest.
t1_why: Depression reduces motivation and pleasure. Small enjoyable actions can slowly rebuild positive feelings and energy.
t1_rem: You don't need to feel motivated first. Action can come before motivation.
t2_preview: Notice harsh thoughts and gently replace them with balanced ones.
t2_why: Depression often creates automatic negative thoughts that feel true but may not be fully accurate.
t2_do3: Ask: "What would I tell a friend?"
t2_do4: Replace it with a kinder, balanced statement
t2_ex_try: "I didn't succeed this time, but I can try again."
t3_preview: Break tasks into very small steps and focus on just one.
t3_why: Large tasks can feel overwhelming. Small goals build confidence and momentum.
t4_preview: Protect your energy by reducing overwhelming situations.
t4_why: When energy is low, too many stressors can worsen symptoms.
t4_do1: Say no to one non-essential task
t4_do2: Take a short break from social media
t4_rem: Protecting your energy is not selfish — it's necessary.
t5_preview: Take care of your basic physical and emotional needs.
t5_why: Sleep, hydration, nutrition, and sunlight strongly affect mood and brain chemistry.
t5_rem: Small physical care can create real emotional change.
sky_intro_1: Imagine your mind as a wide open sky.
sky_intro_2: Thoughts are like clouds passing through.
sky_intro_3: Some are light and fluffy. Some are dark and stormy.
sky_intro_5: Your job is simply to watch them pass.
sky_question: What thought is on your mind right now?
sky_hint: Write one thought that has been bothering you recently.
reflection_hint: How did it feel watching the thought instead of holding onto it?
sell_intro_1: Sometimes our mind tries to sell us thoughts that feel very convincing.
sell_intro_2: In this exercise you will treat your thought like a product someone is trying to sell you.
sell_intro_3: Let's see how valuable it really is.
sell_cost_question: How much would this thought cost?
sell_cost_hint: If someone tried to sell you this thought, how valuable would it actually be?
sell_result_low_desc1: Looks like this thought may not be very valuable.
sell_result_low_desc2: Sometimes our mind tries to sell us things we don't need to buy.
sell_result_mid_desc1: This thought has some pull, but it's not the whole story.
sell_result_mid_desc2: Noticing it is the first step toward gaining perspective.
sell_result_high_desc1: This thought feels convincing right now.
sell_result_high_desc2: Noticing it is the first step toward gaining distance from it.
name_intro_1: Our mind often repeats the same thoughts again and again.
name_intro_2: Instead of fighting them, we can simply name the story our mind is telling.
name_intro_3: This helps us notice the thought instead of getting caught in it.
name_step4_desc1: Next time this thought appears, try saying:
name_step4_phrase_prefix: I'm noticing my mind telling the
name_step4_desc2: This small shift helps you step back from the thought.
conclusion_sky_desc1: Thoughts are like clouds in the sky.
conclusion_sky_desc2: They appear, move across our mind, and eventually pass.
conclusion_sky_desc3: You practiced watching the thought instead of holding onto it.
conclusion_sky_desc4: The more you practice this, the easier it becomes to let thoughts come and go.
conclusion_sell_desc1: Not every thought that appears in our mind deserves our attention.
conclusion_sell_desc2: By evaluating its value, you practiced stepping back and questioning the thought instead of automatically believing it.
conclusion_sell_desc3: Sometimes thoughts sound convincing — but they aren't always worth buying.
conclusion_name_desc1: Our minds often repeat the same stories again and again.
conclusion_name_desc2: By giving the thought a name, you practiced recognizing the story instead of getting caught in it.
conclusion_name_desc3: Next time it appears, you can simply notice:
intro_desc1: Sometimes our thoughts feel overwhelming because we treat them as facts.
intro_desc2: Diffusion techniques help you step back and observe thoughts instead of getting stuck in them.
intro_desc3: In this activity you will try simple exercises that help your mind create space from unhelpful thoughts.
choose_desc: Each exercise helps you look at your thoughts from a new perspective. Pick one and follow the guided steps.
card_sky_desc: Imagine your thoughts drifting across the sky like clouds. Watch them appear and pass by without holding onto them.
card_sell_desc: Treat your thought like something being sold to you. How valuable is it really?
card_name_desc: When thoughts repeat again and again, they become stories. Naming the story helps create distance from it.

watch_the_cloud_move_across_the_sky: Watch the cloud move across the sky.
your_thought_is_simply_passing_through: Your thought is simply passing through.
you_are_the_sky_observing_it: You are the sky observing it.
give_this_thought_a_story_name: Give this thought a story name 📖
what_would_you_call_this_story: What would you call this story?
pick_a_suggestion_or_write_your_own: Pick a suggestion or write your own:
intro_subtitle: Let your brain wiggle for a minute.
intro_reason: Sometimes your brain just needs to move.
intro_benefit: Quick doodles can help release restlessness and reset your focus.
prompt_2: Try filling the space with fun patterns. 🌀
prompt_3: Slow down and draw a calm spiral. 🍥
activity_instructions: Draw anything you want. Try circles, zigzags, spirals, dots, or silly shapes. There are no rules — just keep your hand moving.
end_reset: Even a quick doodle can help your brain reset.
checkin_calmer: Do you feel a little calmer?
checkin_task: Ready to get back to your task?

how_is_energy: How is your energy right now?
msg_very_low: It's okay to feel drained. Listen to your body and prioritize rest.
msg_low: Take it slow. Maybe try a short break or some gentle stretching.
msg_okay: Steady as it goes. Keep monitoring your pace through the day.
msg_good: You're in a good spot! Use this momentum for your tasks.
msg_high: Brilliant! You've got great energy today. Make the most of it!
insight_high: Your energy has been consistently positive this week. It thrives on your current routine!
insight_okay: You've had a steady energy flow. Balancing work and rest seems to be working for you.
insight_low: Your energy has been on the lower side. Consider checking if you're getting enough rest or exercise.
insight_more_data: Continue tracking daily to see deeper patterns in your energy levels.
choose_space: Choose one small space near you.
not_whole_room: Not the whole room — just one small area.
put_away_items: Put away items that belong elsewhere
not_perfect: Stop when the timer ends. It does not need to be perfect.
feel_lighter: Does it feel even slightly lighter?
draining_item: If one item still feels draining, move or remove just that one thing.
welcome_text: Hey… I'm really glad you're here 💛 Let's gently map out what's been going on.
food_patterns_q: Hmm… I hear you. When you feel {{emotion}}… how does food usually show up?
internal_dialogue_q: In those moments… what kind of thoughts pop up?
physical_sensing_q: If you pause for a second… what do you notice in your body?
pattern_desc_1: When you feel {{emotion}}, your body feels {{sensation}}…
pattern_desc_2: …and food becomes a way to {{response}}, while thoughts like "{{thought}}" show up.
support_q: In those moments… what might help you—even a little?
understanding_text: You're understanding yourself a little better. That's more than enough 💛

🗣️ Talk to someone.title: Reach out 💛
🗣️ Talk to someone.body: Sometimes sharing lightens things a bit. Maybe a simple 'hey, can we talk?'

☕ Take a break.title: Pause 💛
☕ Take a break.body: A small pause can help reset things. Maybe step away and take a few slow breaths.

📝 Journal.title: Write it out 💛
📝 Journal.body: You could start with 'right now I feel…' and just let it flow.

🌬️ Breathe.title: Breathe 💛
🌬️ Breathe.body: Inhale 4… hold 2… exhale 6… let's slow it down together.

🎧 Distract myself.title: Gentle shift 💛
🎧 Distract myself.body: A gentle distraction can shift the moment… maybe something light or easy.

screen1_desc: Grief doesn't arrive in neat stages and leave on schedule. It crashes in, retreats, and comes back when you least expect it. That's not a flaw in you — that's grief.
screen1_quote: "There is no right way to grieve. There is only your way."
screen2_desc: This is what people expect grief to look like — vs what it actually is.
screen2_quote: "None of these waves are wrong. All of them are grief."

truths.title: No deadline
truths.body: There's no expiry date on loss. Feeling it months or years later doesn't mean you're stuck — it means you loved.

truths.title: Contradictory feelings
truths.body: You can miss someone and feel relieved. You can laugh and grieve. Grief holds opposites.

truths.title: Love's transformation
truths.body: The pain isn't separate from the love. It's the same thing, transformed.

screen1.intro: The harder you try, the harder it gets.
screen1.title: Your sleep window matters more than you think
screen1.desc: The relationships we choose follow a blueprint written long before we ever fell in love. Familiarity often feels like safety, even when it leads to the same old arguments.
screen1.quote: "Sleep anxiety isn't about not being tired. It's about your nervous system being stuck in 'on' mode."
screen1.p1: A sleep window is the fixed time between when you go to bed and when you wake up. Keeping it consistent — even on weekends — is one of the most powerful things you can do for your sleep.
screen1.p2: This tool helps you build yours in under 2 minutes.
screen1.insight: Even a 1-hour difference in sleep times on weekends can disrupt how you feel all week.

screen2.title: Let's set your sleep window
screen2.desc: The way your caregivers responded to your needs quietly shaped how you relate to others as an adult. These are learned responses — and they can be unlearned.
screen2.quote: "Your brain isn't broken. It has simply learned the wrong lesson about bedtime — and it can be retaught."
screen2.p1: Your amygdala — the brain's threat detector — stays activated at night, signalling danger the moment your head hits the pillow.
screen2.p2: This is called <1>conditioned arousal</1> — it explains why you feel exhausted all day, yet wide awake at bedtime.

step1.title: STEP 1 — WAKE-UP TIME
step1.desc: What time do you need to wake up most days?

step2.title: STEP 2 — SLEEP DURATION
step2.desc: How many hours of sleep do you want?

screen2.italic: Most adults need 7–9 hours. Choose what feels right.

prompts.quote: What does the ground feel like? Grass, sand, water, a wooden floor? Add it below the sky.

prompts.quote: Add one thing that belongs here — a tree, a chair, a window, a pet, a lamp. Anything that feels right.
prompts.hint: It doesn't have to look perfect. It just has to feel true.

prompts.quote: What brings warmth or comfort to this place? A light, a fire, sunlight, a blanket? Add it wherever it feels right.
prompts.hint: Even a small dot of colour counts.

prompts.quote: Finally — add 3 to 5 words that belong here. Tap your drawing to place them anywhere.

benefits_subtitle: When you get your heart pumping, your brain makes some amazing changes. Think of it like upgrading your brain's operating system.
benefits_intro: Right away, your body releases natural mood boosters:
endorphins_desc: Helps reduce pain and boosts mood.

benefits_callout: Just 15 minutes of running can cut your risk of depression by 26%.
benefits_more: That's not all — there's even more happening behind the scenes:
memory_desc: Exercise increases blood flow to the brain, helping improve memory and cognitive function.
stress_desc: Physical activity lowers stress hormones and helps your nervous system reset.

quote: Every journey looks different. Every step forward matters.

stories.name: Aarav
stories.quote: I thought being scared meant something was wrong with me.
body: I didn't know the word "anxiety" when I was younger — I only knew the feeling. My chest would feel tight, my stomach would hurt, and my mind would tell me something bad was about to happen. Even normal days felt dangerous. School mornings were the hardest. I would cling to my parents, afraid that once they left, I wouldn't be okay.

I remember looking at other kids and wondering why they seemed fine. Why didn't their hearts race like mine? Why didn't they want to run home? I felt confused and embarrassed. Sometimes I thought being scared meant I was weak or different in a bad way.

With help, I slowly learned that anxiety wasn't a flaw — it was my brain trying too hard to protect me. Instead of fighting it or hiding from it, I learned small ways to face it. Some days that meant staying in class five minutes longer. Other days it meant just breathing through the fear without running away.
Now I understand something important: courage doesn't mean not feeling scared. It means staying, even when you are. And every time I stay, I grow a little stronger.
stories.reflection: When have you stayed, even when you felt afraid?

stories.name: Daniel
stories.quote: I believed worrying meant I was being responsible.
body: For years, I believed worrying was my job. If I thought through every possible problem, prepared for every outcome, and stayed mentally alert at all times, I could prevent things from going wrong. Anxiety felt productive. It felt responsible.

But over time, I noticed how exhausted I was. My mind never stopped. Even during vacations or family dinners, I was mentally planning, predicting, or preventing. Relaxing felt unsafe. If I stopped worrying, I felt like I was being careless.

Eventually, I realized anxiety wasn't protecting me — it was controlling me. Therapy helped me understand that uncertainty is part of life, and no amount of worrying can eliminate it. I began practicing noticing my thoughts without automatically believing them.

Letting go of constant control felt uncomfortable at first. But slowly, space opened up. I started making decisions based on what mattered to me — not just what felt safest. Anxiety still shows up, but it no longer runs the show.
stories.reflection: Where are you mistaking worry for responsibility?

stories.name: Rhea
stories.quote: I kept waiting to feel confident before living.
body: Anxiety sounded like a voice in my head that never stopped criticizing me. It told me I would embarrass myself, say the wrong thing, or be judged. After conversations, I would replay everything I said. Before events, I would imagine worst-case scenarios.

From the outside, I looked confident. Inside, I felt constantly on edge. I kept waiting to feel ready — waiting for confidence to magically arrive so I could finally speak freely, apply for opportunities, or put myself out there.
But confidence never came first.

What changed my life was realizing I didn't have to eliminate anxiety to move forward. I could bring it with me. I started taking small risks — raising my hand, attending events, speaking up even if my voice shook.
Every time I acted despite fear, something shifted. I stopped measuring myself by how calm I felt, and started measuring myself by how brave I was willing to be.
stories.reflection: What would you try if you didn't wait to feel completely ready?

stories.name: Margaret
stories.quote: I didn't expect anxiety to begin at this stage of life.
body: I thought anxiety belonged to younger people. I didn't expect it to appear after retirement, when life had finally slowed down. But when the busy years ended, my thoughts became louder. Every physical sensation felt alarming. Every unknown felt dangerous.

I felt embarrassed for struggling. At my age, I believed I should have everything figured out. I searched constantly for reassurance — from doctors, from family, from online articles. The reassurance helped briefly, but the fear always returned.

Eventually, I understood that reassurance was feeding the cycle. With guidance, I began practicing sitting with uncertainty instead of chasing certainty. I rebuilt routines. I reconnected with hobbies. I slowly allowed discomfort without immediately trying to fix it.
Today, anxiety still visits sometimes. But it no longer defines me. I've learned that growth doesn't have an expiration date — and courage can appear at any age.
stories.reflection: What part of you is still growing?

stories.name: Kabir
stories.quote: My anxiety never looked like panic. It looked like productivity.
body: I woke up early, worked long hours, and filled every spare moment with something — news, emails, phone calls, background noise. If there was silence, I filled it. If there was stillness, I escaped it.
Because when things got quiet, my thoughts got loud.
What if something happens to my family? What if I lose my job? What if I get sick? The fears weren't constant, but they were persistent. They hovered in the background, waiting for space.
So I gave them no space.
I thought I was just ambitious. Driven. Focused. But underneath the busyness was restlessness. Irritability. A feeling that I could never truly relax.
One evening, my daughter asked me why I was always "somewhere else" even when I was sitting right next to her. That question stayed with me.
I started small. Two minutes of sitting without my phone. No distractions. Just breathing. It felt unbearable at first. My mind raced. My body felt unsettled.
But I kept practicing.
Over time, I learned that thoughts can be loud without being dangerous. I didn't have to solve every fear immediately. I didn't have to outrun discomfort.
Now, I still work hard. I still care deeply. But I also allow moments of stillness. And instead of feeling like a threat, those moments sometimes feel like peace.
stories.reflection: Where in your life are you filling silence to avoid what it might reveal?

stories.name: Nisha
stories.quote: I got promoted — and then couldn't sleep.
body: My anxiety began after something "good" happened.
I got promoted. More responsibility. More visibility. More expectations. Everyone congratulated me. I smiled, thanked them, celebrated — and then went home and couldn't sleep.
What if I'm not actually capable? What if they realize they made a mistake? What if I fail publicly?

The higher I climbed, the more exposed I felt. Every meeting felt like a test. Every small mistake felt like proof that I didn't belong. I started overworking to compensate — staying late, volunteering for extra tasks, answering emails instantly.
From the outside, I looked successful. Inside, I felt like an imposter waiting to be discovered.
The anxiety didn't scream. It whispered. It told me I had to be better than everyone else just to deserve being there. It told me rest was laziness. It told me one wrong move would undo everything.

The breaking point wasn't dramatic. It was a random Tuesday when I forgot a minor attachment in an email — and spent the entire evening replaying it in my head. That's when I realized this wasn't ambition anymore. It was fear.
What slowly changed things was learning that discomfort doesn't mean danger. Growth often feels like exposure. New roles stretch you — they don't mean you're a fraud.
I began allowing myself to be a learner instead of expecting myself to be perfect. I started admitting when I didn't know something. I let myself ask questions.
And something surprising happened.
People respected me more, not less.
Now, when anxiety tells me I don't belong, I don't automatically believe it. I remind myself: being new at something doesn't mean being incapable. It means growing.
And growth has always felt a little uncomfortable.
stories.reflection: Where are you confusing discomfort with proof that you don't belong?

friend.label: Let it go and move on

family.label: Reflect on what you really need

colleague.label: Plan a calmer response next time

other.label: Reach out with a simple message

guided_action.safety_line_default: You don't have to send anything right now. 💛
guided_action.safety_line_reflect: This is just for you—no one else needs to see this. 💛

message.title: Send a Simple Message
message.why: A simple message can break the silence and show the other person you still care.
message.insight: Reaching out—even imperfectly—is almost always received better than we expect.
message.howBody: You don't need to write an essay. A few honest words can open the door to reconnection.
prompts: Hey, I've been thinking about you. Hope you're doing okay.
I know things were tense—just wanted to say I care.
I'm sorry about earlier. Can we talk when you're ready?

acknowledge.title: Acknowledge What Happened
acknowledge.why: Acknowledging what happened shows maturity and helps the other person feel seen.
acknowledge.insight: People don't need you to be perfect—they need to know you noticed.
acknowledge.howBody: You can acknowledge without taking all the blame. Just name what happened honestly.
prompts: I know things got heated between us. I want you to know I noticed.
I realize I may have come across harshly. That wasn't my intention.
I've been reflecting on what happened, and I wish I'd handled it differently.

pause.title: Take a Pause Next Time
pause.why: Committing to a pause gives you space to respond rather than react.
pause.insight: A 6-second pause is often enough to shift from reaction to reflection.
pause.howBody: Think of a simple cue—like taking a breath or stepping away briefly—before responding.
prompts: Next time I feel upset, I'll take a deep breath before responding.
I'll try saying 'Give me a moment' instead of reacting immediately.
I'll step away for a few minutes to collect my thoughts.
pause.promptLabel: Pick a commitment that feels right:

letgo.title: Let It Go for Now
letgo.why: Sometimes the kindest thing you can do is release the weight of the situation.
letgo.insight: Letting go isn't giving up—it's choosing peace over being right.
letgo.howBody: You don't have to forget. Just decide that this doesn't need to control how you feel anymore.
prompts: I'm choosing to let this go—not because it didn't matter, but because my peace matters more.
I release the need to be right about this. I choose connection.
This situation doesn't define our relationship. I'm moving forward.
letgo.editLabel: Write what feels true for you:

reflect.title: Just Reflect
reflect.why: Reflection builds self-awareness, which is the foundation of every healthy relationship.
reflect.insight: Journaling or even thinking through what happened helps you process emotions without pressure.
reflect.howBody: Ask yourself: What was I really feeling underneath the anger? Often it's hurt, fear, or disappointment.
prompts: Underneath my anger, I think I was feeling hurt.
I realize I was reacting out of fear of not being heard.
What I really needed was to feel respected and valued.

you_took_a_small_step_toward_repair_and_: You took a small step toward repair—and that matters.

not_found.title: Not Found
not_found.description: The content you're looking for might have moved.

list.empty_desc: We're currently curating more resources for this topic.

s1.title: Your brain didn't break. It adapted.
s1.description: After trauma, your brain rewires itself to keep you safe. It learned that the world could be dangerous, so it stayed ready. That's not a flaw. That's survival.
s1.placeholder: How do you feel right now?
s1.p1: It's not about <1>how long</1> you sleep.
s1.p2: It's about <1>where</1> in your sleep cycle you wake up.
s1.p3: One thing changes when you understand this — everything.
s1.insight: 💡 Your brain is actually more active during REM sleep than when you're fully awake!
s1.quote: "PTSD isn't a sign that something is wrong with you. It's a sign that something happened to you."

s2.title: Brain survival logic
description: Think of a place where you felt even a little at ease… or a place you wish existed.
Are you sitting, standing, or lying down?
s2.button: So why do I still feel groggy? →
s2.desc: Every 90 minutes, your brain cycles through 4 stages — like a playlist on repeat. Tap each to explore 👇

stages.title: Light sleep
stages.body: You're drifting off. Body starts to let go, muscles may twitch. Very easy to wake up from here.

stages.title: Settling in
stages.body: Heart rate slows, body temperature drops. You're properly asleep — brain activity begins to slow down.

stages.title: Deep sleep ⭐
stages.body: Body repairs tissue, builds immunity. The most physically restorative stage — hard to wake from.

stages.title: REM sleep
stages.body: Brain buzzes with activity. Emotions get processed, memories lock in. Skipping this leaves you foggy.

s2.italic: Deep sleep fixes your body. REM fixes your mind.

truths.body: Hypervigilance and startling easily aren't overreactions — your brain's alarm system is doing its job too well.
Triggers pull your brain back because it never got the signal that the threat was over. It's trying to protect you.
Every symptom was once an act of survival. Your brain did what it had to do to keep you alive.

s2.healing: Healing isn't about forgetting. It's about helping your brain learn that you're safe now.

s3.title: Waking mid-cycle = zombie mode 🧟
description: What's one thing you can see?
Now notice a sound… what do you hear?
s3.button: What does this mean for me? →
s3.p1: Dragged out of deep sleep by an alarm? Your brain literally hasn't finished what it was doing. That foggy heavy feeling? That's <1>sleep inertia</1>.

disruptors.sub: cuts deep sleep by up to 20%
keeps you stuck in light sleep

s3.italic: Your habits directly shape which stages you reach.

s4.title: Which one sounds like you? 👇
description: What is it about this place that makes it feel even a little safe or okay?
also bring your attention to your breath.
s4.notice: If imagining feels hard, just think about the idea of this place.

options.tip: Focus on a consistent sleep and wake time — your body needs a regular rhythm to reach deep sleep.

options.text: I feel emotionally foggy or low
options.tip: Try winding down 30 mins before bed — a calm pre-sleep routine protects your REM sleep.

options.text: I wake up a lot during the night
options.tip: A cool, dark and quiet room helps your brain stay in deeper stages without interruption.

options.text: I feel okay but not great
options.sub: cycles are partial but not complete
options.tip: Try going to bed 20 mins earlier — you may just need one more complete sleep cycle.

s4.quote: Sleep isn't just rest — it's active recovery. Protect your cycles and your whole day changes.

s5.title: Take this with you
description: Notice how you feel right now. Even a small shift matters.
See if you can carry even 5% of this feeling with you.

index.title: Manage Your Stress
index.description: Simple daily habits to help you feel calmer and more in control.

sleep.title: Get Restful Sleep
sleep.description: Aim for 7–9 hours of sleep each night. Try to sleep and wake up at the same time daily.
sleep.why: Sleep helps your brain and body recover and lowers stress hormones.

breathing.title: Practice Deep Breathing
breathing.description: Spend a few minutes focusing on your breath. Slow breathing reduces anxiety.
breathing.why: Slow breathing activates your body's relaxation response.

exercise.title: Move Your Body
exercise.description: Light walking or stretching helps release stress and improve mood.
exercise.why: Exercise releases endorphins and improves mood.

caffeine.title: Limit Caffeine & Sugar
caffeine.description: Too much caffeine and sugar can increase anxiety and disturb sleep.
caffeine.why: Too much caffeine and sugar increases anxiety and sleep problems.
do: Replace one coffee with herbal tea
Avoid caffeine late in the day

planning.title: Plan Your Day
planning.description: Break large tasks into smaller steps to avoid feeling overwhelmed.
planning.why: Planning reduces mental clutter and overwhelm.
do: Focus on one task at a time

scenario.title: What triggers you?
scenario.desc: Pick a situation you relate to

result.title: That's the Pause
result.message: You just created space between what you felt and how you respond. Even a small pause helps you reclaim your center.

scenario_messages.interrupts: Next time someone interrupts you, try this before responding.
scenario_messages.plans: Next time things don't go as planned, try this before reacting.
scenario_messages.ignored: Next time you feel ignored, try this before responding.
scenario_messages.overwhelmed: Next time you feel overwhelmed, try this before doing anything.
scenario_messages.other: Next time you feel triggered, try this before reacting.

result.italic: It won't always feel easy—but consistency is key.

writing.title: Write what feels okay ✨
writing.desc: You can write to your partner, ex-partner, or even to the relationship itself. There's no right or wrong way to do this.
writing.notice: There's no right way to do this. Even a few lines are enough.
writing.subtitle: Your words, your pace

howAreYouFeeling: How are you feeling in this moment?
thankYou: Thank you for pausing and noticing. Even this small check-in matters.
startFirstCheckIn: Start your first check-in to see your journey unfold here.

prompts.supported: What would help you feel supported in this moment?

body_subtitle: Which of these has felt a little off lately? (Select any that apply)
body_options: I eat more than usual when stressed
I haven't been moving or exercising much
I haven't been going outside much
My personal care routine has changed
mind_options: I often imagine the worst-case scenario
I feel guilty about things I've done or didn't do
I find it hard to relax
coping_options: I avoid tasks when I'm stressed
I scroll on my phone a lot to distract myself
I spend time alone to cope
I throw myself into work when stressed
I talk to someone about how I feel
I do breathing exercises to calm down
I watch TV or play games to distract myself
I'm not sure how I respond to stress
question_stress: Which habit increases your stress later?
question_calm: Which one habit could help you feel calmer?
insight_description: Tap below to see simple habits that can help reduce stress.
suggestions: Try going to bed a little earlier tonight
Step outside for fresh air today
Take a break from scrolling for a while
Finish one task you've been putting off
final_text: Small changes matter. Consistency creates calm.

entry.title: Let's take this one step at a time. 💛
description: You don't have to tell your whole story today. Just a small part is enough.
You're safe right now. You're in control of what you write—and what you don't.
entry.breath: Take a slow breath before we begin.
entry.footer: Your words • Your pace • Your story

landing.title: You did something important.
landing.subtitle: Thank you for showing up for yourself.
landing.settle_title: Let's take a moment to settle
steps: Look around and name 3 things you can see.
Take a slow breath in… and out.
landing.quote: "You don't have to carry everything at once. You can come back whenever you feel ready."
